hero.tagline: Where am I? Where am I going? What's the next safe move?
hero.intro: Navigation is the loop a mobile robot runs forever: localize itself, plan a path to a goal, and drive without hitting anything. In ROS 2 that loop is Nav2 - a planner stack that takes a map, a goal, and a steady stream of sensor data, and emits velocity commands. This branch walks the loop end to end: how a differential-drive base actually works, how Bayes filters make sense of noisy sensors, how SLAM builds a map while you drive, how costmaps tell the planner where not to go, and how behavior trees turn it all into a mission. Newcomers should start here right after Setup.

sections.heading: The nav loop
sections.body: Mobile navigation is three concurrent loops feeding each other: a fast local controller (10–20 Hz) that follows a path while dodging dynamic obstacles, a slower global planner (1–5 Hz) that recomputes the route, and a localization filter (continuous) that keeps the robot's pose consistent with sensor data.
bullets: Map - a 2D occupancy grid (or 3D voxel map) of the static world
Localization - AMCL (particle filter) or SLAM running in localization mode
Global planner - A* / Theta* / Hybrid-A* over the costmap
Local controller - DWB, MPPI, or pure-pursuit
Behavior tree - recovery behaviors when the robot is stuck

sections.heading: Mobile robot kinematics
sections.body: Different robots move differently. The kinematic model - how (vx, vy, vθ) relates to wheel velocities - drives every planner and controller you'll write.
bullets: Differential drive (TurtleBot, Roomba) - two driven wheels, can rotate in place, no sideways motion
Ackermann (cars) - front wheels steer, can't rotate in place, has a turning radius
Omnidirectional (Mecanum, omni wheels) - translates in any direction independently of orientation
Holonomic vs non-holonomic - does the robot have constraints on its instantaneous velocity?

codeBlocks.title: Differential drive - body twist → wheel speeds
codeBlocks.code:
def diff_drive_ik(vx, wz, wheel_base, wheel_radius):
    # Ignores vy (diff drive can't sidestep)
    v_left  = (vx - wz * wheel_base / 2) / wheel_radius
    v_right = (vx + wz * wheel_base / 2) / wheel_radius
    return v_left, v_right

sections.heading: Localization - knowing where you are
sections.body: Given a map, localization estimates the robot's pose on it. AMCL (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization) is the ROS standard - it maintains a cloud of pose hypotheses (particles) and weights them by how well their predicted laser scans match the observed scan. After enough motion + observations, the cloud converges to the true pose.

codeBlocks.title: Bring up AMCL with an existing map
codeBlocks.code:
ros2 launch nav2_bringup localization_launch.py \
    map:=$HOME/{{ws}}/maps/my_map.yaml \
    use_sim_time:=false

codeBlocks.title: Set initial pose if AMCL hasn't converged
codeBlocks.code:
ros2 topic pub --once /initialpose geometry_msgs/PoseWithCovarianceStamped \
    "{header: {frame_id: 'map'}, pose: {pose: {position: {x: 0.0, y: 0.0}, \
     orientation: {w: 1.0}}}}"

sections.heading: Bayes filter - predict, then update
sections.body: Every Bayesian state estimator (Kalman, AMCL, MCL, factor graphs) is a discretization of the same loop: predict moves the belief through the motion model, then update reweights it by the sensor likelihood and renormalizes. Try the widget - the corridor has three doors, and your belief starts uniform. Each 'predict' blurs and shifts the belief; each 'sense door' multiplies by p(z|x) and renormalizes. Two or three sense steps separated by predicts collapse the belief onto the true door.
bullets: Predict step is convolution: bel⁻(x) = Σ p(x | u, x') · bel(x'). Information always decreases.
Update step is pointwise multiplication: bel(x) = η · p(z | x) · bel⁻(x). Information increases when the sensor disagrees with motion uncertainty.
Entropy H(bel) is a clean diagnostic: predicts grow it, sensing shrinks it. Watch the H readout in the widget.
AMCL = same loop with particles. Kalman = same loop with Gaussians. Same equations, different representation.
Lost-robot recovery: reset to uniform → after a few sense+predict steps, the cloud collapses on the right door.

sections.heading: Make a map with slam_toolbox
sections.body: When there's no map yet, run SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping). For 2D laser SLAM, slam_toolbox is the modern ROS 2 default - fast, lifelong-learnable, supports map serialization. For RGB-D SLAM (color + depth from a RealSense / Azure Kinect / ZED), RTAB-Map is the dominant open-source choice - mature, ROS 2 packages, used by service-robot vendors worldwide. For 3D LiDAR, LIO-SAM and FAST-LIO are the leaders.

codeBlocks.title: Run online async SLAM
codeBlocks.code:
source ~/{{ws}}/install/setup.bash
ros2 launch slam_toolbox online_async_launch.py \
    use_sim_time:=true

codeBlocks.title: 3D LiDAR - FAST-LIO for spinning Velodyne / Ouster
codeBlocks.code:
cd ~/{{ws}}/src
git clone https://github.com/hku-mars/FAST_LIO.git
cd ~/{{ws}}
colcon build --packages-select fast_lio

sections.heading: Costmaps - turning the map into 'don't go there'
sections.body: Planners don't navigate the raw map; they navigate a costmap. Each cell has a cost - free space (0), inflated obstacle (graded), lethal (≥253). Nav2 builds two costmaps: a global one (static, slow updates) and a local one (rolling window around the robot, fast updates). Layers are stacked: static (the map), obstacle (live laser), inflation (buffer around obstacles), voxel (3D, for arms or cluttered scenes).

codeBlocks.title: Layered costmap config snippet
codeBlocks.code:
global_costmap:
  global_costmap:
    ros__parameters:
      use_sim_time: false
      robot_radius: 0.22
      resolution: 0.05
      track_unknown_space: true
      plugins: ["static_layer", "obstacle_layer", "inflation_layer"]

      static_layer:
        plugin: "nav2_costmap_2d::StaticLayer"
      obstacle_layer:
        plugin: "nav2_costmap_2d::ObstacleLayer"
        observation_sources: scan
        scan: { topic: /scan, max_obstacle_height: 2.0 }
      inflation_layer:
        plugin: "nav2_costmap_2d::InflationLayer"
        cost_scaling_factor: 3.0
        inflation_radius: 0.55

sections.heading: Bring up Nav2 with a simulated robot
sections.body: The fastest way to see Nav2 in action is the TurtleBot 3 simulation. From an empty workspace:

codeBlocks.title: Install + launch
codeBlocks.code:
sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-navigation2 ros-jazzy-nav2-bringup \
    ros-jazzy-turtlebot3-gazebo

export TURTLEBOT3_MODEL=waffle
export GAZEBO_MODEL_PATH=$GAZEBO_MODEL_PATH:/opt/ros/jazzy/share/turtlebot3_gazebo/models

source ~/{{ws}}/install/setup.bash
ros2 launch nav2_bringup tb3_simulation_launch.py headless:=False

sections.heading: Planners and controllers in Nav2
sections.body: Nav2 ships with a smorgasbord of planner and controller plugins. The ones you'll actually use:
bullets: NavFn (default) - Dijkstra-style, fast, good for indoor
Smac (A* / Hybrid A*) - kinematically feasible paths for cars
DWB (default controller) - dynamic window approach, smooth velocity commands
MPPI - sampling-based MPC controller, handles dynamic obstacles + complex constraints
Pure Pursuit - simplest path follower, great for predictable hardware

codeBlocks.title: Switch the local controller to MPPI
codeBlocks.code:
controller_server:
  ros__parameters:
    controller_plugins: ["FollowPath"]
    FollowPath:
      plugin: "nav2_mppi_controller::MPPIController"
      time_steps: 56
      model_dt: 0.05
      batch_size: 2000
      vx_std: 0.2
      wz_std: 0.4
      ax_max: 3.0
      vx_max: 0.5

sections.heading: Try it: A* pathfinding
sections.body: A* expands the open node with the lowest f = g + h, where g is the cost paid so far and h is an admissible heuristic estimating cost-to-go. With a perfect heuristic A* expands only nodes on the optimal path; with a zero heuristic it degenerates to Dijkstra. Drag walls into the grid below and watch the search hug the heuristic gradient toward the goal.
bullets: Open set - frontier of nodes discovered but not yet expanded
Closed set - nodes already expanded; A* will not revisit them
Manhattan heuristic for 4-connectivity is admissible (never overestimates) and consistent
f = g + h is the priority - lowest f wins each iteration
On large maps, swap the linear-scan open set for a binary heap or pairing heap

sections.heading: Send a goal from code
sections.body: RViz is fine for testing, but real behaviors send goals from code. Nav2 exposes an action server you call with a goal pose, and a Behavior Tree you can reshape for complex missions.

codeBlocks.title: send_goal.py - drive to (2.0, 1.5)
codeBlocks.code:
import rclpy
from rclpy.action import ActionClient
from rclpy.node import Node
from geometry_msgs.msg import PoseStamped
from nav2_msgs.action import NavigateToPose

class GoalSender(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('goal_sender')
        self.cli = ActionClient(self, NavigateToPose, 'navigate_to_pose')

    def send(self, x, y, yaw=0.0):
        self.cli.wait_for_server()
        goal = NavigateToPose.Goal()
        goal.pose.header.frame_id = 'map'
        goal.pose.header.stamp = self.get_clock().now().to_msg()
        goal.pose.pose.position.x = x
        goal.pose.pose.position.y = y
        goal.pose.pose.orientation.w = 1.0
        return self.cli.send_goal_async(goal)

def main():
    rclpy.init()
    node = GoalSender()
    fut = node.send(2.0, 1.5)
    rclpy.spin_until_future_complete(node, fut)

sections.heading: Behavior trees - customize the Nav2 mission
sections.body: Nav2 doesn't hardcode 'plan, follow, recover' - it executes a behavior tree (BT) you can edit. The default tree handles 90% of cases (compute path, follow it, on failure trigger recovery, retry), but you can drop in custom nodes for: 'wait for elevator', 'dock at the charger', 'speak to the user', 'pick up trash if seen'. Groot 2 is a visual BT editor that ships alongside.
bullets: BTs are XML - readable, diff-able, swappable at runtime via ROS params
Node types: Action (does work), Condition (returns SUCCESS/FAILURE), Decorator (modifies child)
Control flow: Sequence (all in order), Fallback (try until one works), Parallel
Custom Action nodes - write a C++ class, register it in the BT factory plugin
BehaviorTree.CPP is the underlying library - usable outside Nav2 too

codeBlocks.title: Snippet: add a 'wait for door' check before navigating
codeBlocks.code:
<root main_tree_to_execute="MainTree">
  <BehaviorTree ID="MainTree">
    <Sequence>
      <RetryUntilSuccessful num_attempts="30">
        <IsDoorOpen door_id="front"/>
      </RetryUntilSuccessful>
      <ComputePathToPose goal="{goal}" path="{path}"/>
      <FollowPath path="{path}"/>
    </Sequence>
  </BehaviorTree>
</root>

codeBlocks.title: Point Nav2 at your custom BT XML
codeBlocks.code:
ros2 launch nav2_bringup tb3_simulation_launch.py \
    headless:=False \
    params_file:=$HOME/{{ws}}/config/nav2_params.yaml
# In nav2_params.yaml under bt_navigator:
#   default_nav_to_pose_bt_xml: $HOME/{{ws}}/bts/my_tree.xml

sections.heading: Outdoor & off-road navigation
sections.body: Outdoor robots break every assumption indoor stacks make. GPS works (with RTK for cm accuracy) but drops out under tree canopy. The "free space" is now traversability - a slope you can drive on, mud you can't. 3D LiDAR + dense semantic segmentation feeds a 2.5D elevation/cost map. Specialized stacks: Robotec's Vehicle Dynamics Simulator, Clearpath's outdoor stack, NASA's VIPER lunar rover stack.
bullets: GPS / RTK - absolute reference; falls back to LiDAR-IMU when satellites drop
Traversability - instead of binary obstacle, score each cell by drive-ability
2.5D elevation maps (GridMap) - capture height, slope, roughness per cell
Path-tracking on rough terrain - pure pursuit struggles; switch to MPC
Sensor IP rating - dust, rain, sun glare break consumer cameras quickly

codeBlocks.title: GridMap + traversability example
codeBlocks.code:
sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-grid-map
# Layered map: elevation, slope, friction, semantic
ros2 run grid_map_demos GridMapVisualization \
    --ros-args -p map_topic:=/elevation_map

sections.heading: Social navigation - sharing space with humans
sections.body: A robot moving through a crowd needs more than collision avoidance - it needs to be predictable, polite, and respect social norms. Social Force Models (SFM) treat humans as charged particles; CADRL and SARL apply RL to learn socially-compliant motion. Pedestrian-aware Nav2 plugins (people_msgs, ROS Social Costmap Layer) feed human trajectories into your costmap so the robot slows in crowds and never cuts a pedestrian off.
bullets: Social Force Model - humans + robot as agents with attractive/repulsive forces
CADRL / SARL / NaviGAN - RL policies trained in pedestrian sim
People detection - leg detector (lidar) + YOLO-pose (camera); fuse with EKF
Pedsim_ros - synthetic pedestrian crowd simulator for training/evaluation
Norms: keep > 1m to a human, slow under 0.6 m/s near humans, never cut someone off
